content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 7,
1992
Honorable Bill Leonard
State Senator
Senate Republican Caucus
1020
N Street,
Suite 534
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92—255
Dear Senator Leonard:
This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice regarding the distribution of the “Outlook”
(which is a compilation of various published articles from
newspapers and periodicals)
by the Senate Republican Caucus.
As I discussed with Shirley Seibel of your office on
March 18,
1992,
the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1 does not
prohibit the distribution of the “Outlook.”
In our telephone
conversations of March 18,
1992,
and April 28,
1992,
however,
we
did discuss two potential issues with respect to the distribution
of the “Outlook.”
2
Have Recipients Received a Gift or Contribution?
First,
we discussed whether providing the
“Outlook” to public
officials would be regarded as a gift or contribution to the
officials.
However,
you stated that the “Outlook” was provided
free to any person that requested it,
including public officials,
and therefore did not constitute any special discount to the
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us in
connection with the request.
The Commission does not act as the
finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1 FPPC
Ops.
71.)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916)322-5660


Our File No.
A—92—255
Page
2
officials receiving the publication.
Thus, the “Outlook” would
not be considered a gift.
3
In addition,
you stated that the
publication covered current events and issues in government, but
did not contain campaign infor!aation.
Thus, receipt of the
“Outlook” would not appear to be a contribution.
Is the “Outlook” a Prohibited Mass Mailing?
Secondly,
we discussed the fact that mass mailings at public
expense are now restricted by Section 89001 and Regulation 18901.
However, these provisions only restrict items that meet all of the
following characteristics:
1.
The items are mailed or delivered,
by any means, to a
person’s home, office or post office box.
You stated that the
“Outlook” is mailed.
2.
The cost of the item’s distribution are paid for with
public moneys.
(Regulation 18901(a)(3).)
The “Outlook” is
produced and distributed with public funds.
3.
The item is distributed as a “mass mailing.”
“Mass
mailing” means over two hundred substantially similar pieces of
mail,
but does not include mail which is sent in response to an
unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry.
(Section 82041.5;
Regulation 1890l(a)(4).)
If less than 201 of the same or
substantially similar items are sent in a calendar month,
the
regulation would not restrict the mailing.
You currently mail in
excess of 200 copies of the “Outlook” per month.
4.
The item contains the name, office or title of an elected
official who has acted in cooperation, consultation, coordination
or concert with the agency in producing or distributing the item,
such as Senator Leonard.
(Regulation 18901(a)(2).)
In the
alternative, this factor would be met if the item features an
elected official affiliated with the agency.
This would include
the featuring of any member of the legislature by inclusion of the
legislator’s photograph or signature, or the legislator’s name in
headlines, captions, or unusual type size, typeface,
or type
color.
In our discussion,
you stated that Senator Leonard’s name and
title
4 will not appear in the “Outlook.”
The “Outlook” is
Moreover,
Section 82028(b)
provides that the provision of
informational material to a public official is not a gift.
According to the samples of the publications you submitted with
your request for advice, these materials would be informational as
set forth in the statute.
Please note that the mass mailing restriction would apply if
the Senator’s name, office, title,
or any reference thereto was
included in the mailing.


Our File No.
A—92—255
Page
3
distributed without any cover letter.
In addition, you stated
that no other legislators were cooperating, consulting,
coordinating or acting in concert with the Senator or Senate
Republican Caucus in the production of the newsletter.
Thus,
so
long as other legislators are not featured as set forth above, the
mailing would not fall within the scope of the regulation.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
p-lease feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1..
Sincerely,
SH:JWW:aa
Scott Hallabrin
•ing General Counsel
‘ohn W. Wallace
el, Legal Division
copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of iOc per page.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Senate Republican Caucus may distribute the 'Outlook,' a compilation of published articles, and whether such distribution constitutes a gift, contribution, or prohibited mass mailing under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit distribution of the 'Outlook.' The publication does not constitute a gift or contribution because it is provided free to all requesters without special discount and contains no campaign information. The mailing does not violate mass mailing restrictions if Senator Leonard's name and title do not appear and no other legislators are featured.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks explicit QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains clear legal questions and FPPC advice structured under topical headings. Content and letterhead format confirm this is an FPPC advice letter responding to Senator Leonard's request. Minor extraction uncertainty due to non-standard section formatting.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Senate Republican Caucus may distribute the 'Outlook,' a compilation of published articles, and whether such distribution constitutes a gift, contribution, or prohibited mass mailing under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit distribution of the 'Outlook.' The publication does not constitute a gift or contribution because it is provided free to all requesters without special discount and contains no campaign information. The mailing does not violate mass mailing restrictions if Senator Leonard's name and title do not appear and no other legislators are featured.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms that the Senate Republican Caucus may distribute 'Outlook' (a compilation of published articles) without violating the Political Reform Act, provided that Senator Leonard's name and title do not appear in the publication and no other legislators are featured.
